name: Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop
name_original: Por el canal de Berdún — vuelta por la Jacetania oeste
description: This moderate 20.1-mile touring cycling loop near Berdún offers historical sites and tranquil river valley views along the Aragonese Way.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Canal De Berdún traverse a diverse landscape within the Aragonese Pyrenees, characterized by plains and river valleys. The region is bordered by the Leyre and Orba mountain ranges to the north and the San Juan de la Peña and Monte Oroel ranges to the south, offering varied terrain. The Aragón river flows through the area, featuring extensive riverside woodlands and grasslands. This geographical setting provides a mix of relatively flat routes along the river and more challenging ascents into the mountains. Best touring cycling routes around Canal De Berdún The most popular touring cycling route is Foz de Biniés and Foz de Fago Loop, a 35.5 miles (57.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 4 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores two notable gorges in the area. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop, a moderate 20.1 miles (32.4 km) path. This route follows a section of the historic Camino de Santiago, connecting villages and cultural sites. Local touring cyclists also love the San Juan de la Peña Monastery – View of Monte Oroel loop from Santa Cilia, a 35.8 miles (57.7 km) trail leading through mountain foothills and past significant historical sites, often completed in about 4 hours 10 minutes. Touring cycling around Canal De Berdún is defined by river valleys, historical Camino de Santiago paths, and views of the Pyrenean mountain ranges. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Canal De Berdún are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 23 reviews. More than 364 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Canal De Berdún's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Canal De Berdún?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes around Canal De Berdún. These routes cater to various skill levels, with the majority being classified as difficult, and a few moderate options available.

Q: What are the typical difficulty levels for touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún?
A: The touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún primarily feature difficult terrain, with 18 routes falling into this category. There are also 2 moderate routes available, but no easy routes are listed. This indicates that most cycling here offers a significant challenge.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979929051/foz-de-binies-and-foz-de-fago-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Biniés and Foz de Fago Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979923629/aragonese-way-berdun-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún?
A: The terrain in Canal De Berdún is diverse, ranging from relatively flat routes along the Aragón river valley to more challenging ascents into the Pyrenean and pre-Pyrenean mountain ranges. You'll encounter a blend of plains, river valleys, and mountain foothills, offering varied landscapes for your ride.

Q: What historical and cultural landmarks can I explore along the cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history, especially along the Aragonese Way of the Camino de Santiago. You can visit charming villages like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4612964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ansó&lt;/a&gt; with its medieval architecture, or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5258574" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artieda&lt;/a&gt; known for its traditional mountain buildings. The capital, Berdún, offers noble houses and the Santa Eulalia Church. Significant sites like the Monasteries of San Juan de la Peña and San Salvador de Leyre are also within reach, representing important examples of Aragonese Romanesque art.

Q: Are there any notable natural features or viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The Aragón river itself is a significant feature, with extensive riverside woodlands and pebble beaches. Routes often offer impressive views of the surrounding Pyrenean and pre-Pyrenean mountain ranges, such as the Leyre, Orba, San Juan de la Peña, and Monte Oroel ranges. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979929051/foz-de-binies-and-foz-de-fago-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Biniés and Foz de Fago Loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically explores two notable gorges.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Canal De Berdún?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 23 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, the blend of historical sites with natural beauty, and the quiet mountain passes that allow for an immersive cycling experience.

Q: Is Canal De Berdún suitable for family-friendly touring cycling?
A: While the region offers beautiful scenery, many of the touring cycling routes are classified as difficult, with no easy routes listed. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979923629/aragonese-way-berdun-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option that follows a historic path, which might be more suitable for families with older, experienced cyclists. However, families with young children or beginners might find the terrain challenging.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Canal De Berdún?
A: The region's dynamic river path, shaped by precipitation and snowmelt, suggests that spring and early summer would offer lush scenery and moderate temperatures. Autumn would also be pleasant with cooler weather and potentially vibrant foliage. Winter cycling might be challenging due to snow in higher elevations.

Q: Are there options for parking near the touring cycling trails?
A: While specific parking areas for bikes are not detailed, the villages and towns along the routes, such as Berdún, Ansó, Artieda, and Puente la Reina de Jaca, are likely to offer parking facilities. These locations serve as common starting points for many tours.

Q: Can I access touring cycling routes in Canal De Berdún using public transport?
A: The Canal de Berdún region, particularly villages like Puente la Reina de Jaca, has historically been an important crossroads. While specific public transport links for cyclists are not detailed, it's advisable to check local bus services that connect the main towns and villages in the area, as these might offer access points to the routes.

Q: Which route offers a good balance of historical sites and natural beauty?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31511307" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan de la Peña Monastery – View of Monte Oroel loop from Santa Cilia&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. This difficult route leads through mountain foothills and past significant historical sites, including the famous monastery, while offering impressive views of Monte Oroel.
tips: Also a nice gravel road to ride with your bike :). The road is in good condition!

If you are doing the Aragonese Way and take the detour to this municipality, you will soon understand where the name comes from. Puente la Reina de Jaca has been an important crossroads for centuries and a crossing point on the Camino de Santiago since medieval times. While only a few ruins of the old bridge remain, a new one was built in the 20th century. The municipality has all kinds of services.

Biniés is a small town governed by its old castle which is currently preserved and where it is privately owned. The center is located on an alluvial terrace above the Veral river, which comes from the heart of the Pyrenees. Village with history.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle route follows the Aragón river valley, offering a mix of gently rolling and sometimes nearly flat sections. While some parts are well-paved, you might encounter unpaved or gravel segments, especially along the Ribera río Aragón. The broader Aragonese Way, of which this loop is a part, combines fields and forests with open spaces, providing varied riding conditions.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in beautiful and varied landscapes. The route passes through charming forests and small villages, showcasing medieval fortresses, Romanesque architecture, and traditional settlements like Biniés. The journey is steeped in medieval history and offers wonderful natural surroundings, including views of the Pyrenees in the distance.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Berdún Loop?
faqs.answer: The ideal time to cycle this route is between April and October. Snowfall can make parts of the broader Aragonese Way impassable from November to May, so planning for the warmer months ensures the best conditions for your ride.

faqs.question: Is the Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The loop is rated as 'moderate' for touring bicycles. While the broader Aragonese Way is considered demanding, this specific loop's distance and elevation gain are manageable for cyclists with some experience. However, be prepared for varied terrain, including potential unpaved sections.

faqs.question: Where can I find parking for the Berdún Loop?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for the Berdún Loop is not detailed, but as the route passes through towns like Puente la Reina de Jaca and Berdún, you can typically find public parking in or near these settlements. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is the Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is generally open, specific rules for dogs can vary. When passing through villages or on private land, dogs should be kept on a leash. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them. Consider the terrain and distance for your dog's capabilities.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a special permit or to pay an entrance fee to cycle the Aragonese Way: Berdún Loop. It is a public route, part of the Camino de Santiago network.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is part of the historic Camino de Santiago. It also passes through sections of the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse, Viejo Camino de Santiago por el Valle de Hecho - Calzada del Summo Pyreneo, and various segments of the Camino de Santiago Aragonés.

faqs.question: Are there accommodation options or places to eat along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, despite being a less-traveled path, the route passes through towns and villages where you can find pilgrim hostels, cafes, and restaurants. Towns like Berdún and Puente la Reina de Jaca offer options for food, drink, and overnight stays. However, accommodation may be sparse in certain low-traffic areas, so planning ahead is recommended.

faqs.question: What makes this loop unique compared to other routes in the region?
faqs.answer: The Berdún Loop offers a unique blend of tranquility and historical immersion. As a less-traveled section of the Camino de Santiago, it provides a more peaceful experience away from crowds, allowing for a deeper appreciation of the landscapes and medieval sites. It's an excellent choice for those seeking a rewarding touring bicycle experience with a rich cultural backdrop.

faqs.question: How can I reach the starting point using public transport?
faqs.answer: Public transport options to the Berdún area are limited but available. Regional bus services connect larger towns in Aragón, such as Jaca, which may have onward connections to smaller villages near the loop. It's best to check current bus schedules for routes serving Berdún or nearby settlements like Puente la Reina de Jaca.
